Package javashooter.controller

Class ObjectController

java.lang.Object
javashooter.controller.ObjectController
Direct Known Subclasses:
KinematicsController, LevelController, LimitedTimeController, Simple4WayController
public abstract class ObjectController extends Object
Class that controls the LOGICAL behavior of an object independently of how it is displayed or drawn. The most important method here is updateObject(): this method is, by various indirections, called exactly once per game time step for every object that is on the javashooter.playground. It has, by virtue of the member variable gameObject (of type GameObject), full access to
  • the object it is controlling
  • the javashooter.playground this object belongs to
Typically, updateObject would check whether an object leaves the screen to react appropriately. In that case the object can be marked for deletion (by adding it to the flag "deleted" that is always defined for any javashooter.playground), but of course other reactions are possible like rebounding, emerging on the other side, ...

    • receive

      public void receive(String msg)
      Confirms receipt of a broadcast message by an object.
      Parameters:
      message - The message that was received.

    • MessageHasReceived

      public boolean MessageHasReceived(String msg)
      Checks if a specific message has been received by an object.
      Parameters:
      message - The message to check for.
      Returns:
      true if the message has been received, false otherwise.

    • cleanupBroadcasts

      public void cleanupBroadcasts()
      Clears all processed broadcast messages to free up resources.

    • updateObject

      public abstract void updateObject()
      Is called once every game time step by the game itself. NEVER call this directly, not necessary!
      The method can do whatever it likes, including nothing. The field gameObject contains a reference to the controlled object.

    • applySpeedVector

      public void applySpeedVector()
      Convenience method: simply moves the object forward one step from its present position, using its present speed.
